proposals.title: Non-Resonant Exceptional Points in Continuum Wave Scattering from Anisotropic Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Exceptional points (EPs) in non-Hermitian photonics enable extraordinary phenomena like unidirectional invisibility and enhanced sensing. However, current realizations predominantly rely on resonant structures (e.g., coupled cavities or guided modes), which are inherently narrowband and sensitive to fabrication imperfections. A platform for achieving EPs in a broadband, non-resonant scattering regime would unlock robust applications in optical isolation, thermal management, and wavefront control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that EPs can be engineered in the scattering continuum by tailoring the non-Hermitian and anisotropic properties of a deeply subwavelength metasurface, without employing any resonant cavity or bound mode, through precise control of its non-local spatial dispersion.
proposals.method: We will design a planar, bianisotropic metasurface using subwavelength, non-resonant meta-atoms (e.g., twisted metallic patches or dielectric pillars) to provide tailored electric, magnetic, and magnetoelectric coupling. The scattering matrix of the metasurface will be analytically derived and tuned via its effective surface susceptibility tensor. By leveraging a plane-wave scattering formalism, we will identify EP conditions where eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the scattering matrix coalesce for specific incident angles and polarizations. Full-wave simulations and parameter retrieval will guide the fabrication of a silicon-based prototype, with EP verification performed via polarization-resolved angular scattering measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for non-Hermitian photonics by demonstrating EPs in a purely non-resonant, scattering-based system. It will provide a design framework for broadband, angular-dependent exceptional points, directly applicable to creating ultra-thin optical isolators and arbitrarily polarized thermal emitters without relying on resonant quality factors.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by deliberately avoiding resonant modes or cavities, which are the cornerstone of all pre-2026 EP demonstrations, and instead engineering EP degeneracies directly in the open scattering channels of a metasurface.

proposals.title: Exceptional-Point-Enhanced Thermal Emission Sensors with Robust Polarization Control
proposals.motivation: Photonic sensors operating near non-Hermitian exceptional points (EPs) promise orders-of-magnitude sensitivity enhancement to perturbations, but their real-world application is hindered by fabrication disorder and environmental fluctuations that detune the system from the exact EP. This severely limits their practical utility. A robust platform is needed that maintains EP-enhanced sensitivity under realistic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a judiciously designed thermal metasurface, leveraging the interplay between tailored non-Hermitian couplings and the broadband nature of thermal emission, can sustain robust EP-enhanced sensing by averaging over a continuum of states, thereby mitigating the sensitivity to single-frequency detuning.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a coupled-resonator metasurface supporting a polarization EP in its scattering matrix. The design will utilize anisotropic, temperature-tunable materials (e.g., VO2, GST) in a geometry supporting quasi-bound states in the continuum (BICs) to achieve high-Q resonances. Robustness will be engineered by creating a spectral continuum of near-EP conditions across the thermal emission band. Sensing will be performed by monitoring the dramatic, topologically protected polarization shift in the far-field thermal emission pattern in response to a target analyte, using a custom polarization-resolved thermal imaging setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first experimental demonstration of an EP-based sensor that maintains enhanced sensitivity in the presence of realistic fabrication imperfections and thermal drift. It will establish a new paradigm for robust non-Hermitian sensing by merging concepts from thermal photonics, metasurfaces, and singularity optics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using the inherent spectral breadth of thermal emission, typically a drawback for coherent sensors, as a resource to average over disorder and stabilize the EP condition, a concept unexplored in pre-2026 EP sensor research which focused on narrowband laser-driven systems.

proposals.title: Generalized Scattering Matrix Framework for Designing and Probing Non-Hermitian Photonic Systems at Exceptional Points
proposals.motivation: Exceptional points (EPs) in non-Hermitian photonics enable unprecedented control over light, including unidirectional invisibility and enhanced sensing. However, their design and analysis in complex, tunable metasurfaces—a core interest of Prof. Alù's group—often relies on Hamiltonian models that are cumbersome for predicting far-field scattering responses and device-level performance. A robust, circuit-inspired framework is needed to bridge modal non-Hermitian physics with measurable input-output characteristics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generalized scattering (S-) matrix and impedance formalism, adapted from microwave network analysis, can provide a unified and experimentally actionable model for designing photonic metasurfaces operating at EPs, directly linking their singular eigenvalue degeneracies to far-field polarization and emission patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop a multiport S-matrix model for bianisotropic metasurfaces, treating meta-atoms as lumped non-Hermitian circuit elements. The method will involve extracting effective impedance parameters from full-wave simulations, constructing the system's S-matrix, and analytically enforcing the coalescence of both eigenvalues and eigenvectors (the EP condition) as constraints on matrix elements. This will be applied to design thermal metasurfaces and magnetless isolators from recent group work, using the framework to predict and optimize unidirectional emission and isolation bandwidth.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a powerful, transferable design tool for non-Hermitian photonic devices, moving beyond modal analysis to a port-based description familiar to engineers. It will directly demonstrate how to tailor far-field wavefronts—such as arbitrarily polarized thermal emission—by operating at an EP, offering a new pathway to extreme optical functionality.
proposals.novelty: While S-matrices are standard in photonics, their explicit formulation to impose and exploit exceptional point degeneracies for far-field control in metasurfaces is novel, merging microwave network synthesis techniques with cutting-edge non-Hermitian optics.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Non-Hermitian Metasurfaces for Tailored Exceptional Point Phenomena
proposals.motivation: Exceptional points (EPs) in non-Hermitian photonic systems enable extraordinary optical responses, such as unidirectional invisibility and chiral mode switching. However, current EP-based devices are designed through intuition and parameter sweeps, limiting the complexity and functionality achievable. An inverse-design framework is needed to systematically discover nanophotonic structures that realize prescribed EP degeneracies with targeted spectral and polarization properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gradient-based topology optimization, constrained by non-Hermitian Hamiltonian models, can discover metasurface geometries that host EPs with designer spectral positions, polarization responses, and dispersion, surpassing the capabilities of intuitive designs.
proposals.method: We will implement a computational inverse-design pipeline combining a non-Hermitian coupled-mode theory (CMT) surrogate model with full-wave adjoint-based optimization. The CMT model, parameterized by metasurface geometry, will provide efficient gradients of eigenvalues and eigenvectors with respect to design variables. A topology optimization algorithm, using a density-based method and the method of moving asymptotes (MMA), will iteratively adjust a silicon-on-insulator metasurface's unit cell to minimize a loss function targeting a specific EP order and its associated scattering matrix singularity. Validation will be performed via rigorous coupled-wave analysis (RCWA) and finite-element method (FEM) simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust computational tool for generating EP-based metasurface blueprints with unprecedented control over non-Hermitian degeneracies. It will produce specific device designs for applications in arbitrarily polarized thermal emission control and robust optical sensing, directly extending the group's recent work on thermal metasurfaces. The methodology will establish a new paradigm for designing non-Hermitian photonic systems.
proposals.novelty: While inverse design is established for Hermitian photonics, its application to *intentionally* create and tailor exceptional points—where eigenvalues and eigenvectors coalesce—is a novel and non-trivial extension. This moves beyond using EPs as a serendipitous outcome to treating them as a direct, optimizable objective.

proposals.title: Exceptional Points in Terahertz Metasurfaces for Non-Hermitian Polaritonic Control
proposals.motivation: Exceptional points (EPs) in non-Hermitian photonic systems have enabled remarkable phenomena like asymmetric mode conversion and enhanced sensing, but demonstrations have been largely confined to the optical and near-infrared regimes. Realizing EPs at terahertz (THz) frequencies would unlock these functionalities for critical applications in high-speed communications, non-destructive testing, and molecular fingerprinting, where THz waves are uniquely suited but active control remains a challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the non-Hermitian coupling between surface plasmon polaritons and quasi-bound states in the continuum in a planar metasurface, we can robustly create and tune exceptional points in the terahertz spectral range.
proposals.method: We will design a bilayer metasurface using twisted anisotropic meta-atoms (inspired by recent work on twisted Weyl semimetals) to provide the necessary non-Hermitian coupling. The structure will consist of a patterned graphene layer atop a dielectric on a metallic ground plane, enabling gate-tunable loss and coupling rates. We will employ full-wave finite-element simulations to map the parameter space (twist angle, Fermi energy, geometry) to locate EPs, followed by fabrication via electron-beam lithography and characterization using terahertz time-domain spectroscopy with a polarization-resolved setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first experimental demonstration of a fully planar, actively tunable EP system at THz frequencies. It will establish a new platform for non-Hermitian THz photonics, providing a pathway to components like ultrasensitive sensors, topological circulators, and lasers with engineered linewidths in this technologically important band.
proposals.novelty: While EPs are known, their realization in a scalable, planar THz architecture using gate-tunable polaritonic metasurfaces is novel. This approach merges concepts of non-Hermitian physics with the practical need for active THz wavefront control, moving beyond static, three-dimensional resonator geometries.

proposals.title: Orbital-Selective Scattering Metasurfaces for Label-Free Molecular Fingerprinting
proposals.motivation: Selective light-matter interaction at the molecular orbital level remains a frontier in nanophotonics. While metasurfaces can control scattering and emission, they typically interact with broad electronic or vibrational bands. Pre-2026 work on thermal Rashba effects and unidirectional emission demonstrates control over polarization and direction, but not the selective resonant coupling to specific, narrow electronic transitions within a molecule. Achieving such orbital selectivity would enable unprecedented control over molecular signatures for sensing and spectroscopy.
proposals.hypothesis: A metasurface composed of non-degenerate, high-quality-factor resonators can be designed such that its split resonant modes spectrally align with distinct orbital energy levels of a target molecule, leading to orders-of-magnitude enhancement in the Raman or Rayleigh scattering cross-section of a single selected transition while suppressing others.
proposals.method: We will design a dielectric metasurface using broken-symmetry silicon meta-atoms to create spectrally sharp, non-degenerate electric and magnetic dipole resonances. Full-wave simulations (FDTD, Lumerical) will optimize the geometry to match the resonance splitting to the energy difference between specific molecular orbitals (e.g., in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons). The design will incorporate a twisted lattice or gradient phase to leverage the thermal Rashba effect principles, enabling directional scattering of the amplified signal. Fabrication will use standard silicon-on-insulator lithography, followed by characterization using spatially resolved Raman spectroscopy with tunable excitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm of 'orbital-selective scattering,' moving beyond bulk material response to active control of specific quantum transitions. It will deliver a foundational platform for ultra-sensitive, label-free chemical detection without fluorescent tags and provide a new method for dynamically engineering the spectral and directional radiative signature of composite material systems.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the concept of resonant mode splitting for orbital matching with the goal of *enhanced scattering* (not absorption or transparency), and integrates directional emission control from thermal metasurface concepts into a scattering-based spectroscopic tool, which is non-obvious from prior isolation or thermal emission work.

proposals.title: Direct Wavefront Engineering via Nonlocal Metalines: Eliminating Graded-Index Superstrates for Compact Beam Shaping
proposals.motivation: Current metaline-based devices for wavefront shaping, such as beam deflectors or lenses, often require an integrated graded-index (GRIN) superstrate to impart the necessary phase profile, adding complexity, thickness, and fabrication challenges. A design paradigm that achieves arbitrary wavefront control using only the metaline array would enable ultra-compact, planar photonic systems with simplified integration. This is critical for advancing applications in on-chip optics, LiDAR, and ultra-thin metasurface optics where footprint and fabrication yield are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically engineering the nonlocal interactions between meta-atoms within a single-layer metaline, we can synthesize the full 2π phase coverage and requisite spatial gradient for wavefront shaping without relying on a GRIN lens or superstrate for phase accumulation.
proposals.method: We will design Huygens' meta-atom unit cells supporting overlapping electric and magnetic dipole resonances to achieve high transmission with full phase control. A coupled-dipole model will be used to map and optimize the nonlocal near-field couplings between adjacent meta-atoms along the line. An inverse-design algorithm, specifically an adjoint-based optimization, will be employed to iteratively adjust the geometry and placement of each meta-atom to directly produce target wavefronts (e.g., focusing, deflection) from a normally incident plane wave, using the metaline as the sole functional element. Prototypes will be fabricated for optical wavelengths and characterized using near-field scanning optical microscopy (NSOM) and far-field diffraction measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design framework for single-layer, nonlocal metalines that perform complex beam shaping, moving beyond the need for auxiliary phase-modifying layers. It will deliver experimentally validated devices demonstrating high-efficiency deflection and focusing, directly contributing to the miniaturization of planar optical systems. The principles will expand the toolkit for metasurface engineering by leveraging designed nonlocality as a resource.
proposals.novelty: While nonlocal effects are often considered parasitic, this proposal deliberately exploits them as the primary mechanism for phase engineering within a single layer, a significant departure from the standard approach of using a metaline merely as a grating coupler to a separate GRIN medium. The direct synthesis of a focusing phase profile without a refractive index gradient in the superstrate is a non-obvious and unexplored capability.

proposals.title: Generalized Wavefront Processing with Nonlocal Metalines for Arbitrary Phase Profiles
proposals.motivation: Metasurfaces have revolutionized wavefront control for planar incident waves, enabling flat optics. However, real-world sources and complex scattering environments often generate non-planar wavefronts with arbitrary phase profiles, which conventional local-phase-gradient metasurfaces cannot process correctly. Extending metasurface functionality to operate on such complex incident fields is crucial for applications in computational imaging, adaptive optics, and communication with distributed sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing nonlocal metalines—metasurface strips with tailored spatial dispersion—we can perform specified linear operations (e.g., differentiation, integration, spatial filtering) on incident waves with arbitrary, non-planar phase profiles, independent of the specific incident field shape.
proposals.method: We will design metalines using a coupled-mode theory framework extended to handle nonlocal interactions, optimizing for a target Green's function in the spatial frequency domain. The design will be implemented using high-index dielectric resonators (e.g., silicon nanopillars) on a substrate, with coupling engineered via asymmetric lattice arrangements and inter-element distances. Full-wave simulations (FDTD, Lumerical) will validate designs for operations like spatial differentiation on Gaussian-beam and scattered-wave incidences, followed by experimental characterization at near-infrared wavelengths using a phase-sensitive spatial light modulator to generate complex test wavefronts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for wavefront processing that is decoupled from the incident field's phase profile, moving beyond the planar-wave assumption. It will provide a foundational framework and experimental proof-of-concept for 'field-agnostic' metasurface operations, directly impacting computational sensing and optical signal processing.
proposals.novelty: While metasurfaces are known for shaping outgoing waves, performing invariant operations on arbitrary incoming wavefronts using nonlocal metalines is a significant and unexplored generalization, distinct from previous work on isolated functions like polarization sorting or unidirectional emission for specific incidences.

proposals.title: Analog Optical Computing with Low-Loss, Weakly Confined Modes in Nonlocal Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Analog optical computing using metasurfaces promises ultra-fast, parallel mathematical operations for tasks like image differentiation and equation solving. However, implementations relying on highly confined surface plasmons suffer from significant Ohmic losses, limiting computational fidelity and scalability. This work addresses a critical bottleneck by exploring a low-loss paradigm shift, moving away from extreme field confinement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging weakly confined, nonlocal optical modes in judiciously designed metasurfaces, it is possible to implement key analog computing kernels—such as spatial differentiation and integration—with drastically reduced propagation losses while maintaining sufficient momentum-space selectivity for effective wave-based computation.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate dielectric metasurfaces supporting guided-mode resonances and Bloch modes, which exhibit weaker field confinement than plasmons but lower absorption. Using Fourier-plane analysis, we will engineer the angular-dependent complex transmission matrix of the metasurface to perform desired mathematical operations on an incident optical field. Specific computational tasks, like edge detection via first-order spatial differentiation, will be implemented by tailoring the Pancharatnam-Berry phase and resonant dispersion of the unit cells. Performance will be characterized by comparing the processed output intensity profiles for structured light inputs against ideal results, quantifying the trade-off between computational accuracy and insertion loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design framework for low-loss, on-chip analog optical processors, directly quantifying the performance gains over plasmonic systems. It will expand the toolkit of metasurface-based computing by introducing nonlocal, weakly confined modes as a viable and efficient platform, potentially enabling more complex, cascadable optical computing circuits.
proposals.novelty: While metasurface-based analog computing is known, the systematic exploitation of weakly confined modes specifically to circumvent the fundamental loss-confinement trade-off of plasmons is a novel and non-obvious pathway. This approach merges concepts from nonlocal flat optics with analog computation, a synergy not yet fully explored in existing literature.

proposals.title: Broadband Topological Wavefront Shaping with Dispersion-Engineered Metaline Arrays
proposals.motivation: Metasurfaces and their one-dimensional counterparts, metalines, have revolutionized wavefront control at optical frequencies, but their operation is typically limited to a narrow bandwidth due to resonant phase-shifting elements. This severely restricts their utility in applications requiring polychromatic light, such as broadband imaging, spectroscopy, and white-light photonic systems. Achieving robust, multi-wavelength functionality with a single, planar interface remains a critical, unsolved challenge in nanophotonics.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically combining multiple, dispersion-engineered meta-atoms within a single supercell and leveraging concepts of synthetic dimensions and topological robustness, we can design metalines that maintain prescribed wavefront-shaping functions—such as beam steering or focusing—across a broad, continuous range of optical wavelengths.
proposals.method: We will design metaline supercells comprising a sequence of dielectric or plasmonic meta-atoms with tailored, complementary spectral phase responses. A multi-objective genetic algorithm will optimize the geometry of these elements to achieve a target linear or parabolic phase profile across a target bandwidth (e.g., 1200-1800 nm). The design will be validated using full-wave finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations. Fabrication will employ electron-beam lithography on silicon-on-insulator or III-V semiconductor platforms, with characterization via spatially resolved spectral interferometry to map the scattered wavefronts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general design framework for broadband, non-resonant metalines, moving beyond single-frequency operation. It will demonstrate experimentally validated devices for broadband beam deflection and focusing, directly applicable to on-chip spectrometers and broadband holograms. The project will also provide new insights into managing dispersion in low-dimensional metasystems.
proposals.novelty: While broadband metasurfaces exist, the explicit design of one-dimensional metalines for multi-wavelength operation is largely unexplored. The approach is novel in its application of topological concepts for bandwidth robustness in a line array and its direct connection to Andrea Alù's recent work on polarization-independent and unidirectional thermal emission from metasurfaces.

proposals.title: Spatial Optical Computing with 2D Nonlocal Metaline Transmit-Arrays
proposals.motivation: Optical analog computing offers a path to ultra-fast, low-power image processing tasks like edge detection and convolution, crucial for machine vision and sensing. Current demonstrations using metasurfaces are often limited to 1D operations or rely on local phase profiles, restricting their functionality to simple spatial filtering. A platform capable of performing arbitrary, programmable 2D kernel operations on an optical field in a single pass would represent a significant advance for integrated photonic signal processing.
proposals.hypothesis: By extending the concept of nonlocal metalines—which perform mathematical operations via guided-mode coupling along one dimension—into a 2D array of coupled resonators, we can create a transmit-array that implements a designed 2D spatial impulse response, enabling complex image transformations directly in the optical domain.
proposals.method: We will design a 2D metaline array as a planar lattice of subwavelength silicon nitride resonators, where nearest-neighbor coupling in both transverse dimensions is precisely engineered via evanescent fields. The target 2D convolution kernel will be mapped onto the array's nonlocal interaction matrix using an inverse-design algorithm based on coupled-mode theory and gradient optimization. The device will be fabricated using standard lithography and characterized by projecting structured light patterns onto the array and measuring the transmitted field with a spatially resolved detector to validate operations like Laplacian and Gaussian filtering.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental platform for 2D nonlocal optical analog computing using a planar metasurface, moving beyond 1D differentiation or integral transforms. It will provide a general framework for mapping mathematical kernels onto engineered nonlocal light-matter interactions, bridging the fields of nanophotonics and optical information processing.
proposals.novelty: While 1D metalines for spatial differentiation exist, the extension to a fully 2D, nonlocal, and reconfigurable transmit-array for arbitrary kernel operations is unexplored. The novelty lies in the synthesis of a 2D coupled-system response to perform general linear transformations on an optical image, a significant leap from the single-axis operations of prior metasurface computing devices.

proposals.title: Broadband Dispersion Engineering of Spoof Surface Plasmon Polaritons via Nonlocality in Gradient Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Spoof surface plasmon polaritons (SPPs) enable subwavelength confinement and manipulation of electromagnetic waves at lower frequencies, but their dispersion is typically narrowband and fixed by the geometry. A method to dynamically control spoof SPPs over a wide, continuous bandwidth is lacking, limiting their application in integrated photonic circuits, broadband sensing, and reconfigurable antennas. This proposal addresses the critical need for broadband spoof SPP manipulation, a key challenge in planar plasmonics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a judiciously designed gradient metasurface, leveraging nonlocal interactions between meta-atoms, can provide continuous control over the spoof SPP dispersion relation, enabling broadband wavefront shaping and unidirectional launching. The nonlocal response will break the typical trade-off between bandwidth and control efficiency inherent in locally resonant designs.
proposals.method: We will design a planar metasurface composed of subwavelength, anisotropic metallic patches on a textured metal-dielectric substrate to support spoof SPPs. The design will implement a spatially varying gradient in the patch geometry (e.g., size and orientation) to impart a tailored momentum kick. We will employ full-wave finite-element simulations (e.g., COMSOL) coupled with an analytical coupled-mode theory framework to model the nonlocal interactions. The metasurface will be fabricated using standard lithography, and its performance will be characterized using a near-field scanning microwave microscope to map the spoof SPP field distribution across a multi-octave bandwidth (e.g., 5-40 GHz).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for broadband spoof plasmonic devices, moving beyond narrowband resonant control. It will deliver a concrete metasurface platform for broadband spoof SPP beam steering, focusing, and isolation. The results will directly impact the development of compact, planar components for next-generation wireless and sensing systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in exploiting engineered nonlocality in a gradient metasurface to achieve continuous bandwidth control of spoof SPPs, a significant departure from existing methods based on discrete, resonant unit cells. This approach merges concepts of dispersion engineering from photonics with the subwavelength confinement of plasmonics in a uniquely broadband manner.

proposals.title: Planar Hyperlens for Subwavelength Imaging via Spoof Surface Plasmon Polariton Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Conventional optics are diffraction-limited, preventing imaging of features smaller than half the wavelength. While metamaterial hyperlenses can overcome this limit, they are typically bulky, three-dimensional, and challenging to fabricate and integrate. A planar, on-chip solution is critically needed for applications in nanoscale bio-imaging, semiconductor inspection, and integrated photonics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a planar metasurface, engineered to support spoof surface plasmon polaritons (SPPs) with a hyperbolic dispersion, can act as an effective hyperlens, transferring subwavelength object details into the far field as propagating waves.
proposals.method: We will design a planar metal-dielectric metasurface on a silicon substrate, with subwavelength metallic corrugations to create spoof SPPs. The unit cell geometry (e.g., groove depth, period) will be tailored using full-wave simulations (e.g., finite-element method in COMSOL) to achieve a hyperbolic iso-frequency contour. We will numerically and experimentally characterize the dispersion and test the lens by imaging a subwavelength slit array at mid-infrared wavelengths (~10 µm), analyzing the transmitted field profile and resolution enhancement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first experimental demonstration of a fully planar hyperlens based on spoof SPPs, directly translating a powerful metamaterial concept into a manufacturable, integrable platform. It will establish a new design paradigm for sub-diffractional optical components that are compatible with standard nanofabrication processes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the concepts of spoof plasmon metasurfaces—a mature platform for surface wave control in the Alù group—with the hyperlens functionality, creating a planar device rather than a volumetric metamaterial, which is a significant departure from prior implementations.

proposals.title: Volumetric Spoof Plasmonic Metamaterials for Three-Dimensional Subwavelength Wave Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Spoof surface plasmon polaritons (SPPs) on structured metallic surfaces enable subwavelength confinement and control of electromagnetic waves, but their functionality is inherently limited to 2D interfaces. Extending this powerful concept into a 3D bulk medium would unlock unprecedented control over wave propagation in all three spatial dimensions, enabling novel volumetric devices for imaging, sensing, and wavefront engineering that are impossible with planar metasurfaces alone.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing and fabricating a three-dimensional lattice of subwavelength metallic resonators with tailored inter-unit coupling, we can create a bulk volumetric metamaterial that supports spoof SPP modes propagating within its volume, exhibiting anomalous dispersion and extreme field confinement not found in natural materials.
proposals.method: We will design the unit cell as a 3D metallic 'molecule' (e.g., a split-ring resonator or a connected helix) using full-wave electromagnetic simulations (CST Microwave Studio, COMSOL). These cells will be arranged in a cubic lattice via 3D nanoprinting (two-photon polymerization followed by metallization). We will characterize the bulk dispersion via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and near-field scanning optical microscopy (NSOM) to map the subwavelength modal profiles. The coupling between adjacent layers will be tuned by varying the lattice constant and unit-cell orientation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental realization of a true volumetric spoof SPP medium, providing a new material platform for 3D subwavelength optics. It will deliver a fundamental understanding of inter-layer coupling in plasmonic metamaterials and demonstrate prototype devices such as volumetric hyperlenses or 3D waveguides with arbitrary bending loss below the diffraction limit.
proposals.novelty: While 2D spoof SPPs on corrugated surfaces are well-studied, creating a bulk 3D equivalent where spoof plasmonic modes propagate volumetrically, not just at an interface, is a non-trivial and unexplored leap. This moves beyond stacking decoupled 2D metasurfaces to engineer a new class of bulk electromagnetic materials with designed volumetric mode structures.

proposals.title: Inverse-Designed Metasurface Couplers for Arbitrary Far-Field Radiation from Spoof Surface Plasmon Polaritons
proposals.motivation: Spoof surface plasmon polaritons (SPPs) offer subwavelength confinement and waveguiding at terahertz and lower frequencies, but their efficient conversion into designed free-space beams remains a challenge. Current couplers are largely ad-hoc, limiting the potential of integrated spoof SPP circuits for advanced beam steering, holography, and compact antenna applications. A systematic design framework is needed to bridge the gap between confined near-fields and arbitrarily structured far-field radiation.
proposals.hypothesis: That a passive, planar metasurface, designed via an inverse optimization process, can efficiently couple a confined spoof SPP mode to a pre-specified, complex free-space radiation pattern with independent control over polarization and directionality.
proposals.method: We will formulate the design as an inverse scattering problem, coupling a single-mode spoof SPP waveguide to a metasurface composed of subwavelength, anisotropic meta-atoms. A gradient-based optimization algorithm, such as adjoint-based topology optimization, will be implemented to iteratively tailor the local phase, amplitude, and polarization response of each meta-atom. The objective function will directly maximize coupling efficiency into a target far-field pattern, defined by a superposition of plane waves, while suppressing back-scattering into the SPP mode.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general inverse-design methodology for SPP-to-free-space conversion, moving beyond simple out-coupling. It will demonstrate, experimentally at microwave or terahertz frequencies, metasurface couplers that produce vortex beams, multi-beam steering, and arbitrarily polarized emission directly from a guided spoof SPP mode. This bridges the fields of plasmonics, metamaterials, and antenna engineering.
proposals.novelty: While inverse design has been applied to free-space metasurfaces and SPP excitation, its application to the *out-coupling* of a *single guided spoof SPP mode* into an *arbitrary user-defined far-field pattern* is unexplored. This approach treats the metasurface as a leaky-wave antenna with a fundamentally non-local excitation, requiring a new optimization framework.

proposals.title: Loss-Compensated Non-Hermitian Metasurfaces for Arbitrary Wavefront Control
proposals.motivation: Metasurfaces enable unprecedented control over light, but their performance in applications like thermal emission shaping and optical isolation is fundamentally limited by intrinsic absorption and scattering losses. These losses constrain efficiency, bandwidth, and the realization of ideal non-reciprocal or extreme wave-manipulating responses. Integrating optical gain offers a pathway to overcome these limitations, moving from passive, loss-limited devices to active, high-performance systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically integrating optically pumped gain media within the sub-wavelength unit cells of dielectric metasurfaces, we can achieve loss compensation or even net gain for specific resonant modes, thereby enabling high-efficiency, arbitrary wavefront transformation and unidirectional emission previously unattainable with passive structures.
proposals.method: We will design dielectric metasurfaces (e.g., silicon nitride or titanium dioxide nanopillars) supporting high-quality-factor Mie resonances at near-infrared wavelengths. Gain will be introduced via an organic dye (e.g., IR-140) or colloidal quantum dots embedded in a polymer matrix surrounding the meta-atoms. Full-wave simulations (FDTD, Lumerical) coupled with a four-level gain model will optimize the geometry and gain distribution. Fabricated samples will be characterized via pump-probe spectroscopy and near-field scanning optical microscopy to map the local field enhancement and quantify the compensation of radiative and non-radiative losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general framework for designing and fabricating active, loss-compensated metasurfaces. It will demonstrate a critical step towards ideal non-Hermitian optical systems, directly impacting fields like thermal photonics (enabling brighter, more directive thermal sources) and integrated photonics (enabling low-loss, on-chip wavefront shaping).
proposals.novelty: While gain in metamaterials has been explored for negative-index media, its integration into *dielectric* metasurfaces for arbitrary wavefront control—specifically linking it to thermal emission and polarization sorting functionalities pioneered by the PI—is unexplored. The novelty lies in the co-design of gain and geometry to preserve phase and polarization control while suppressing loss.

proposals.title: Topology-Optimized Metasurfaces for Arbitrary Wavefront and Polarization Control of Thermal Emission
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work has demonstrated that thermal metasurfaces can break traditional constraints on emission, enabling unidirectional or polarized thermal radiation. However, designing structures for complex, multi-functional emission profiles—such as simultaneous control of directionality, polarization, and spectral selectivity—remains a significant challenge. Inverse design offers a powerful, systematic route to discover non-intuitive, high-performance geometries that are difficult to conceive with intuition-based approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying gradient-based topology optimization to the design of nonlocal metasurfaces will yield novel, manufacturable geometries that achieve previously unattainable combinations of thermal emission properties, such as generating multiple, independently controlled beams with distinct polarizations from a single thermal source.
proposals.method: We will implement a computational framework coupling full-wave electromagnetic solvers (e.g., rigorous coupled-wave analysis) with gradient-based topology optimization (using the method of moving asymptotes). The design region will be a thin-film semiconductor (e.g., silicon) layer on a substrate, parameterized by a continuous density variable. The objective function will target specific far-field Stokes parameters across desired angles and wavelengths, incorporating constraints for fabrication feasibility. The adjoint method will be used to compute gradients efficiently for the coupled thermal and electromagnetic response.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a general inverse-design methodology for thermal metasurfaces, producing specific device blueprints for advanced thermal management and infrared signaling. It will expand the catalog of achievable thermal emission phenomena, moving beyond single-function devices to demonstrate integrated, multi-channel thermal emitters.
proposals.novelty: While topology optimization has been used for passive optics, its application to the design of *thermal* metasurfaces—where the source is embedded and the structure itself is at equilibrium—is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in optimizing the nonlocal, complex scattering matrix of the metasurface to sculpt the incoherent emission from a broadband thermal source, a far more challenging problem than shaping a known external beam.

proposals.title: Nonreciprocal Photonic Transport via Spatiotemporally Modulated Degenerate Resonances
proposals.motivation: Achieving optical nonreciprocity without magnetic fields or nonlinearities is a central challenge in integrated photonics. Current dynamic modulation schemes, such as spatiotemporal modulation, fundamentally rely on lifting mode degeneracy to break Lorentz reciprocity, imposing strict design constraints and limiting material platforms. Exploring whether this strict requirement can be relaxed would unlock new pathways to nonreciprocal devices using simpler, more symmetric resonant structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the spatiotemporal symmetry of the modulation waveform itself, nonreciprocal coupling and isolation can be achieved even between degenerate optical modes, which are traditionally considered unsuitable for such dynamic schemes.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate silicon-based metasurfaces supporting degenerate quasi-bound states in the continuum (BICs) at near-infrared wavelengths. A traveling-wave modulation will be induced via patterned, radio-frequency-driven carrier injection in a PIN diode architecture integrated into the metasurface plane. We will theoretically analyze the system using a coupled-mode theory framework extended to degenerate subspaces under modulation, and experimentally characterize nonreciprocity by measuring the contrast in transmission for forward and backward excitation using a heterodyne interferometric setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for nonreciprocal photonic devices, demonstrating that mode degeneracy is not a fundamental barrier to isolation. It will expand the toolkit for magnet-free nonreciprocity, potentially leading to more compact and efficient optical circulators and isolators that leverage high-Q degenerate resonances.
proposals.novelty: The approach is non-obvious because all prior dynamic nonreciprocity schemes explicitly require nondegenerate modes; here, we intentionally exploit degeneracy and control it via the modulation's spatiotemporal pattern, a paradigm shift from existing methods.

proposals.title: Nonreciprocal Optomechanical Metasurfaces via Spatiotemporal Modulation
proposals.motivation: Achieving nonreciprocal light propagation without magnetic fields is a fundamental challenge in integrated photonics. While static metasurfaces and optomechanical systems offer control over wavefronts and frequency conversion, they typically lack the dynamic, direction-dependent response needed for optical isolation on-chip. Spatiotemporal modulation in silicon photonics has demonstrated nonreciprocity, but its application to optomechanical platforms remains unexplored, limiting the potential for low-loss, magnet-free isolators and circulators.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a traveling-wave spatiotemporal modulation to an optomechanical metasurface, we can break Lorentz reciprocity, enabling nonreciprocal transmission and unidirectional amplification of light at the interface. This will create a dynamically reconfigurable optical diode effect directly from the interaction of light with coherently driven mechanical modes.
proposals.method: We will design a silicon-on-insulator metasurface with subwavelength resonators coupled to GHz-frequency mechanical modes via radiation pressure. A phased array of integrated piezoelectric actuators will apply a coherent, traveling-wave modulation to the mechanical resonators, creating a synthetic momentum bias. We will simulate the system using coupled-mode theory and finite-difference time-domain methods to model the non-Hermitian band structure. Experimental validation will involve fabricating the device and characterizing its S-parameters using a heterodyne interferometry setup to measure forward/backward transmission contrast.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for nonreciprocal optics by merging spatiotemporal modulation with optomechanics, leading to compact, magnet-free isolators. It will extend the functionality of thermal and polarization-control metasurfaces to include active, direction-dependent routing, advancing towards fully integrated nonreciprocal photonic circuits.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in transferring the traveling-wave modulation concept from pure photonic waveguides to an optomechanical metasurface platform, creating nonreciprocity through a synthetically moving mechanical landscape rather than electronic modulation or nonlinearities.

proposals.title: Brillouin-Active Metasurfaces for Tailored Phonon Emission and Nonreciprocal Thermal Transport
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work in non-Hermitian and topological photonics has demonstrated exquisite control over light at interfaces, including unidirectional thermal emission. However, the active engineering of thermal phonon emission and transport, which is crucial for thermal management and energy conversion, remains a significant challenge. Stimulated Brillouin scattering (SBS) offers a powerful mechanism to couple light to coherent acoustic phonons, but its integration with planar metasurface platforms for tailored phonon engineering is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a Brillouin-active medium with a judiciously designed thermal metasurface, we can achieve stimulated emission of coherent acoustic phonons with tailored momentum and polarization, enabling nonreciprocal control over phonon transport and near-field radiative heat transfer.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate metasurfaces from low-symmetry, anisotropic materials (e.g., α-MoO3, van der Waals crystals) known to support hyperbolic phonon polaritons, deposited on a Brillouin-active chalcogenide glass waveguide. Using a dual-pump SBS configuration, we will generate coherent, counter-propagating acoustic waves at the metasurface interface. The metasurface's geometric and modal dispersion will be engineered via full-wave simulations (e.g., finite-difference time-domain methods) to phase-match the SBS-generated phonons to specific thermal emission channels, manipulating their directionality and polarization as recently demonstrated for photons.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for active phonon engineering at the nanoscale, moving beyond passive thermal control. It will demonstrate the first metasurface-based Brillouin phonon laser and provide a foundational platform for studying nonreciprocal phonon transport, with direct applications in directional cooling, topological phononics, and enhanced optomechanical sensing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the fields of stimulated Brillouin scattering and thermal metasurface physics to create a *coherent* phonon source with metasurface-defined properties, a significant leap from prior work on passive control of thermal photon emission.

proposals.title: Bulk Topological Transport in Large-Scale 3D Metamaterial Lattices with Engineered Weyl Points
proposals.motivation: Topological photonics, inspired by electronic systems, has demonstrated robust waveguiding in 2D metasurfaces. However, the extension to 3D bulk topological metamaterials, particularly those hosting Weyl points, remains a significant challenge due to fabrication complexity and the need for large-scale arrays to observe genuine bulk transport phenomena. Realizing such systems would unlock unprecedented control over light, enabling backscattering-immune propagation in three dimensions and novel light-matter interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by assembling a large 3D lattice of magnetless, bianisotropic unit cells with broken inversion symmetry, we can synthesize and observe robust bulk topological transport of electromagnetic waves, mediated by pairs of ideal Weyl points in momentum space.
proposals.method: We will design a 3D unit cell based on twisted, resonant metallic or dielectric elements, inspired by prior work on twisted Weyl semimetals, to induce the necessary bianisotropic response. Using full-wave simulations (CST, COMSOL), we will optimize the lattice to open a topological bandgap and create Weyl points. The structure will be fabricated via multi-layer stacking or advanced 3D printing (e.g., two-photon polymerization). Transport will be characterized using a near-field scanning system with a microwave or terahertz source to map field propagation through the bulk of a large-scale (e.g., >20x20x20 unit cell) array, comparing topological and trivial phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental demonstration of bulk topological wave transport in a 3D, large-scale, on-chip compatible metamaterial. It will establish a scalable platform for studying 3D topological physics with light and validate design principles for magnetless topological materials. The results will directly impact the development of robust photonic circuits and novel antennas.
proposals.novelty: While 2D topological edge states are well-studied, this proposal focuses on the non-trivial challenge of scaling to a 3D bulk lattice to observe transport *through* the topological medium itself, moving beyond surface phenomena. It leverages magnetless designs from metasurface work to achieve 3D topological properties without external magnetic fields.

proposals.title: Nonreciprocal Phononic Transport via Optically Driven Spatiotemporal Modulation in Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Nonreciprocal control of mechanical waves (phonons) is a fundamental challenge in classical wave physics, with applications in vibration isolation, acoustic logic, and thermal management. While magnet-based acoustic isolators exist, they are bulky and incompatible with integrated systems. Recent advances in spatiotemporal modulation of optical metasurfaces for breaking Lorentz reciprocity offer a promising, magnetless pathway that has not been translated to the phononic domain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying dynamic spatiotemporal modulation—inspired by magnetless photonic isolators—to a carefully designed elastic metasurface, we can break time-reversal symmetry for mechanical waves, achieving strong nonreciprocal transmission and isolation in the ultrasonic to gigahertz regimes.
proposals.method: We will design a piezoelectric metasurface lattice where the effective stiffness of its resonant elements is modulated via time-varying electrical bias, creating a traveling-wave-like perturbation in the material properties. The modulation parameters (frequency and phase gradient) will be optimized using coupled-mode theory and full-wave elastic finite-element simulations (COMSOL Multiphysics) to maximize the nonreciprocal bandgap. Experimental validation will use laser Doppler vibrometry on a fabricated lithium niobate metasurface prototype, with the modulation signal applied via integrated electrodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental demonstration of magnetless, broadband nonreciprocity for surface acoustic waves using spatiotemporal modulation. It will provide a foundational framework for on-chip phononic isolation and routing, directly impacting integrated phononic circuits and coherent phonon manipulation.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by directly translating the concept of spatiotemporal modulation from optical metasurfaces to the elastic domain, creating a dual to optical isolation, and by achieving this in a monolithic, actively tunable platform without requiring moving parts or nonlinear saturation.

proposals.title: Coherent Perfect Absorption via Time-Reversed Thermal Emission from Non-Hermitian Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Coherent perfect absorbers (CPAs) are the time-reversed counterpart to lasers, enabling complete light absorption at a critical coupling condition. Realizing practical, on-chip CPAs for arbitrary input polarizations and angles remains a significant challenge in integrated photonics and sensing. This project addresses this by inverting the design principles of recent demonstrations of tailored, polarized thermal emission from metasurfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a non-Hermitian metasurface, designed by time-reversing the conditions for unidirectional and arbitrarily polarized thermal emission, can function as a broadband, polarization-insensitive coherent perfect absorber for incident coherent waves.
proposals.method: We will design a bilayer plasmonic metasurface supporting quasi-bound states in the continuum (q-BICs) to engineer critical coupling. The structure will be optimized using full-wave finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations to match its complex scattering matrix poles and zeros, ensuring time-reversed lasing conditions. We will fabricate the design using electron-beam lithography on a silicon-on-insulator platform and characterize it using a tunable laser coupled to a fiber interferometer to provide the required coherent, counter-propagating input beams.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel metasurface architecture for coherent perfect absorption that is robust to input polarization and angle. It will establish a direct experimental link between the principles of tailored thermal emission and coherent absorption, advancing the field of non-Hermitian photonics for optical signal processing and energy harvesting.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying the time-reversal concept specifically to metasurfaces engineered for arbitrary polarized thermal emission, moving beyond canonical CPA designs to create devices with inherent polarization diversity and angular robustness.

proposals.title: Active Chiral Thermal Metasurfaces for Tailored Molecular Excitation
proposals.motivation: Chiral light sources are crucial for probing molecular chirality in spectroscopy and sensing, but generating intense, tunable, and directionally controlled circularly polarized thermal emission remains a challenge. Recent work on thermal metasurfaces has demonstrated control over polarization and directionality of thermal emission, yet active, on-demand generation of pure chiral states from a thermal platform is unexplored. This capability would bridge nanophotonics and molecular spectroscopy, enabling new tools for chiral analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a thermally driven, non-Hermitian metasurface, engineered with judiciously tailored nonlocal and asymmetric scattering matrices, can act as an active source of spectrally selective and directionally biased chiral thermal emission. This emission can be tuned to resonantly excite specific chiral molecular modes.
proposals.method: We will design a planar silicon-based metasurface supporting quasi-bound states in the continuum (BICs) with tailored in-plane asymmetry to induce a controlled chiral near-field. The structure will be integrated with a microheater for active thermal excitation. We will employ full-wave finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations coupled with fluctuational electrodynamics (the scattering matrix method) to model the thermal emission properties. The design will be optimized using gradient-based topology optimization to maximize the degree of circular polarization (DCP) and directivity at target mid-infrared molecular fingerprint wavelengths.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first active, thermally driven chiral light source based on a metasurface, providing a new paradigm for generating chiral fields without external optical pumping or magnetic fields. It will deliver a theoretical framework and experimental demonstration for tailoring thermal chiral emission, directly impacting molecular spectroscopy and chiral sensing technologies.
proposals.novelty: While chiral sensing with metasurfaces is known, inverting the process to create a *thermal* chiral emitter is a fundamentally new concept. The novelty lies in merging active thermal control, non-Hermitian physics for unidirectional emission, and chiral near-field engineering into a single planar platform for targeted molecular excitation.

proposals.title: Experimental Demonstration of Tailored Wavefront Shaping via Epsilon-Near-Zero Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Epsilon-near-zero (ENZ) materials offer a unique platform for extreme wave manipulation, enabling deep subwavelength light confinement and phase accumulation. While theoretical and numerical studies (pre-2026) predict that patterning the ENZ layer can sculpt the phase of transmitted or reflected light with unprecedented spatial resolution, experimental validation of this phase-pattern control in the optical domain remains a critical gap. Demonstrating this would unlock practical, ultra-compact devices for beam steering, holography, and optical signal processing.
proposals.hypothesis: By lithographically patterning a deeply subwavelength, optically thin ENZ film (e.g., ITO or AZO) into a metasurface with spatially varying geometric parameters, we can experimentally realize and measure designer, discontinuous phase profiles across an optical interface, thereby validating the concept of ENZ phase tailoring.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ENZ metasurfaces using electron-beam lithography and reactive ion etching on commercially available ITO films, targeting their ENZ wavelength near 1.55 µm. The metasurface unit cells will consist of nanoscale apertures or pillars whose dimensions are varied to encode a target phase gradient, such as for beam deflection. Characterization will involve spectroscopic ellipsometry to confirm the effective ENZ response and a custom-built near-field scanning optical microscope (NSOM) combined with Fourier-plane imaging to directly map the amplitude and phase of the transmitted wavefront, comparing results to full-wave simulations (e.g., FDTD).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence of designed phase front engineering using a patterned ENZ layer, moving beyond effective medium descriptions. It will establish a fabrication and characterization protocol for ENZ metasurfaces and demonstrate a functional optical element, such as a beam deflector or lens, whose operation is fundamentally tied to the ENZ condition.
proposals.novelty: While metasurfaces using high-index resonators are well-established, this approach leverages the singular phase response of a *patterned effective zero-index film*, where phase shifts are accrued over deeply subwavelength distances, offering a distinct physical mechanism and potential for more compact, dispersion-engineered devices compared to conventional dielectric or plasmonic metasurfaces.

proposals.title: Modal Phase Engineering via Epsilon-Near-Zero Plasmonic Cavities for Arbitrary Wavefront Control
proposals.motivation: Epsilon-near-zero (ENZ) materials exhibit extreme field enhancement and wave slowing, enabling ultra-subwavelength confinement. However, a unified framework linking their modal phase properties to established cavity physics is lacking, limiting their systematic use in wavefront shaping. This project aims to bridge this gap by treating ENZ regions as effective plasmonic cavities, providing a powerful design principle for metasurfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an ENZ region can be modeled as an ultra-subwavelength plasmonic cavity whose resonant modes possess a quantized, tailorable phase profile. This modal phase can be directly mapped to the far-field radiation phase of a metasurface element, enabling arbitrary wavefront control.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate metasurfaces where each meta-atom incorporates a tailored indium tin oxide (ITO) ENZ cavity at its telecom-range ENZ wavelength. Full-wave simulations (Lumerical FDTD) will analyze the complex eigenmodes and their phase distribution. We will employ scattering matrix and eigenmode expansion techniques to extract the effective cavity length and modal phase. Experimental validation will involve near-field scanning optical microscopy (NSOM) to map the local phase and Fourier-plane imaging to characterize the far-field wavefront.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new cavity-based paradigm for understanding and designing ENZ-based meta-atoms, moving beyond effective medium theory. It will deliver a set of design rules for modal phase control, directly applicable to creating metasurfaces for arbitrarily polarized and unidirectional emission, as well as optical isolation devices.
proposals.novelty: While ENZ phenomena are well-studied, conceptualizing them as quantized plasmonic cavities to engineer modal phase is a novel approach. This directly connects the deep-subwavelength modal properties to macroscopic wavefront engineering, a non-obvious synthesis of plasmonic cavity concepts and ENZ physics.

proposals.title: Large-Aperture Beamforming and Steering with Epsilon-Near-Zero Metasurfaces for RF and Microwave Systems
proposals.motivation: Epsilon-near-zero (ENZ) materials and metasurfaces have demonstrated extraordinary wavefront manipulation capabilities at optical frequencies, enabling sub-wavelength phase control and arbitrary polarization shaping. However, scaling these effects to the radio frequency (RF) and microwave regimes for practical, large-scale antenna systems remains a significant challenge. Realizing this scale-up would unlock transformative applications in next-generation communications, radar, and sensing by enabling ultra-compact, low-profile, and highly reconfigurable beamforming apertures that circumvent the bulk and complexity of traditional phased arrays.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing and fabricating planar, large-area metasurfaces with tailored spatial dispersion to achieve an effective ENZ condition at RF/microwave frequencies, we can implement precise, sub-wavelength phase profiling across an electrically large aperture to control far-field radiation patterns with unprecedented efficiency and compactness.
proposals.method: The approach will involve the design of passive, non-resonant unit cells based on deeply sub-wavelength metallic patterning on low-loss dielectric substrates to create a locally tunable effective permittivity profile. We will employ full-wave electromagnetic simulations (e.g., CST, HFSS) coupled with retrieval methods to map geometric parameters to effective medium properties. A gradient-descent or adjoint optimization algorithm will be used to inversely design the spatial distribution of unit cells across a meter-scale aperture to implement specific phase masks for beam steering and shaping. The metasurface will be fabricated using standard printed circuit board (PCB) lithography and experimentally characterized in an anechoic chamber with a vector network analyzer.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a practical framework for implementing ENZ-based wavefront manipulation in scalable RF hardware. It will deliver a functional prototype demonstrating efficient (>70%) beam steering over a ±60° range without moving parts or complex feeding networks. The results will bridge the gap between nanophotonic concepts and macro-scale radio engineering, providing a new paradigm for flat antenna technology.
proposals.novelty: While ENZ phenomena are well-studied in optics, their application to electrically large, practical radiating systems in the RF domain is unexplored. The novelty lies in translating sub-wavelength optical phase control to a scale orders of magnitude larger, using a non-resonant, dispersion-engineered metasurface approach to overcome the bandwidth and loss limitations typical of resonant metamaterials.

proposals.title: Programmable Spatiotemporal Metasurfaces for Adaptive Non-Reciprocal Sound Control
proposals.motivation: Static acoustic metamaterials offer fixed wave-manipulation properties, limiting their utility in dynamic environments. Recent demonstrations of magnetless non-reciprocity via spatiotemporal modulation in optics and acoustics, including Alù's work on twisted Weyl semimetals and thermal emission control, have opened a path for dynamic devices. However, achieving real-time, programmable switching of non-reciprocal acoustic functions remains a significant challenge for adaptive sound isolation, cloaking, and routing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subwavelength acoustic metasurface, composed of actively tunable resonant elements whose properties are modulated in time via a programmable digital control system, can achieve on-demand, high-contrast switching between distinct non-reciprocal wave-manipulation states, such as isolation, focusing, and steering.
proposals.method: We will design a planar array of piezoelectric Helmholtz resonators, each shunted by a field-programmable gate array (FPGA)-controlled circuit enabling rapid modulation of effective acoustic impedance. The FPGA will implement synchronized, traveling-wave-like spatiotemporal modulation profiles based on pre-calculated phase sequences (e.g., Floquet protocols) to break time-reversal symmetry. Real-time switching will be achieved by uploading different modulation waveforms (e.g., varying phase velocity or amplitude) to the FPGA, dynamically reconfiguring the metasurface's scattering matrix. Experiments will characterize non-reciprocal transmission and reflection in a waveguide setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first experimental platform for software-defined, adaptive acoustic non-reciprocity, moving beyond single-function demonstrations. It will establish a design framework for spatiotemporal metasurfaces with multiple, switchable operational modes, directly impacting the development of intelligent acoustic systems for noise control, imaging, and communications.
proposals.novelty: While spatiotemporal modulation for non-reciprocity is known, the integration of real-time digital programmability to achieve *multiple, switchable* acoustic functions on a single, static hardware platform is novel and non-trivial, merging concepts from active metamaterials with control systems engineering.

proposals.title: Nonreciprocal Metasurface Sensors for Invisible Probing of Complex Wavefronts
proposals.motivation: Metasurface-based sensors and isolators are typically designed and characterized for ideal, coherent plane-wave illumination. However, realistic optical fields in complex media, such as biological tissue or disordered photonic structures, are diffuse, scattered, and possess intricate wavefronts. This mismatch limits the practical application of advanced metasurface concepts in real-world sensing and imaging scenarios where the incident field is unknown and non-ideal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a nonreciprocal, magnetless metasurface sensor can be designed to perform a specific, localized measurement (e.g., of a field component or spatial derivative) while remaining effectively invisible to the complex ambient wavefield, by exploiting tailored scattering cancellation and momentum-dependent nonreciprocal response.
proposals.method: We will design a metasurface unit cell combining a twisted Weyl semimetal element (for magnetless nonreciprocity) with a carefully tailored bianisotropic response. The design will be optimized using inverse-scattering algorithms (e.g., coupled dipole method with a nonreciprocal Green's function) to achieve a near-zero scattering cross-section for a broad angular and spatial frequency spectrum, representing diffuse fields, while maintaining a strong, nonreciprocal coupling to a specific local field observable. Full-wave simulations (FDTD, FEM) will model performance under synthesized complex wavefronts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for sensors that operate in complex wave environments without perturbing them, moving beyond plane-wave assumptions. It will provide a theoretical framework and experimental blueprint for noninvasive probes in biomedical optics, near-field microscopy, and wave physics in disordered systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the concepts of scattering cancellation (optical invisibility) and magnetless nonreciprocity specifically for the purpose of sensing, targeting complex wavefronts rather than simple plane-wave isolation, which is a significant departure from existing metasensor and isolator designs.

proposals.title: Active Loss Compensation and Non-Hermitian Wave Control in Acoustic Metasurfaces via PT-Symmetric Gain-Loss Modulation
proposals.motivation: Acoustic metamaterials and metasurfaces offer unprecedented control over sound waves, but their performance is fundamentally limited by intrinsic material and radiative losses. Pre-2026 research in optics has demonstrated that parity-time (PT) symmetric systems, with balanced gain and loss distributions, can enable exceptional phenomena like loss-induced transparency and unidirectional invisibility. Translating these concepts to acoustics remains a significant challenge, crucial for realizing high-efficiency, non-reciprocal acoustic devices and robust wave manipulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing an acoustic metasurface with PT-symmetric gain-loss modulation—achieved through active piezoelectric elements and tailored resistive networks—we can actively compensate for intrinsic losses and achieve regimes of acoustic non-Hermitian physics, such as PT-symmetry breaking and exceptional points, for enhanced wave control.
proposals.method: We will design a planar acoustic metasurface unit cell integrating a piezoelectric membrane (PZT) coupled to an active electronic feedback circuit to provide controllable acoustic gain, balanced against a passive lossy element. The PT-symmetric condition will be enforced by arranging these gain and loss units in a one-dimensional lattice with subwavelength spacing. We will use transfer matrix methods and finite-element simulations (COMSOL) to model the non-Hermitian scattering matrix and predict the exceptional point. Experimental characterization will involve measuring the complex transmission/reflection spectra and near-field pressure profiles under swept-frequency excitation from an acoustic plane wave source in an anechoic chamber.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first experimental demonstration of active loss compensation in an acoustic metasurface via PT symmetry, moving beyond passive loss mitigation. It will establish a design framework for non-Hermitian acoustic systems, enabling new functionalities like loss-immune focusing and non-reciprocal sound manipulation without bulky magnetic biases, advancing towards practical, efficient acoustic devices.
proposals.novelty: While PT symmetry is well-studied in optics, its application to actively compensate for losses in *acoustic metasurfaces* is novel. The proposed integration of active piezoelectric gain units into a subwavelength planar architecture to achieve and exploit exceptional points for sound control is a non-obvious extension of Alù's group's expertise in optical non-Hermitian photonics and thermal metasurfaces to the acoustic domain.

proposals.title: Tailored Nonlocal Dispersion Engineering via Detuned Plasmonic-Intersubband Polaritons
proposals.motivation: Strong coupling between localized plasmonic resonances and intersubband transitions (ISBTs) in semiconductor heterostructures has enabled ultrastrong light-matter interactions and novel mid-infrared sources. However, this coupling inherently locks the hybrid polariton dispersion to a specific, fixed spectral overlap, limiting dynamic control over the optical response. The ability to independently tune and detune these two resonant phenomena would unlock new regimes of nonlocal dispersion engineering, crucial for active metasurfaces and tunable nonlinear optics.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing a voltage-tunable semiconductor quantum well design and a spatially decoupled plasmonic metasurface, we can create a system where the plasmonic resonance and ISBT are independently tunable, enabling a transition from strong coupling to weak coupling and the exploration of tailored, highly nonlocal dispersion relations not accessible in locked systems.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a hybrid platform featuring a III-V semiconductor heterostructure with multiple quantum wells, where the ISBT energy is electrically tuned via the quantum-confined Stark effect. This active layer will be separated by a nanoscale dielectric spacer from a patterned gold metasurface supporting a designer plasmonic resonance. Full-wave electromagnetic simulations (using finite-element methods in COMSOL) will map the parameter space of spacer thickness and detuning. Experimental characterization will involve Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and angle-resolved reflection measurements to reconstruct the polariton dispersion under varying bias voltages.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for active dispersion control in the mid-infrared, moving beyond fixed strong coupling. It will provide a foundational platform for voltage-tunable optical isolation, reconfigurable thermal emission, and dynamically controlled nonlinear frequency conversion, directly extending concepts from Dr. Alù's group on thermal metasurfaces and magnetless isolation to active, nonlocal systems.
proposals.novelty: While strong plasmonic-ISBT coupling is well-studied, the deliberate and dynamic *detuning* of these resonances to engineer specific nonlocal dispersion features is unexplored. This approach leverages independent electrical and geometric tuning knobs to access new optical regimes beyond the typical polariton picture.

proposals.title: Giant and Tunable Nonlinearities via Intersubband Transition Coupling to Bound States in the Continuum in Dielectric Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Intersubband transitions (ISBTs) in quantum wells offer giant optical nonlinearities but are typically coupled to plasmonic resonances, leading to high Ohmic losses that limit device efficiency and practical applications. Recent advances in low-loss dielectric Mie-resonant metasurfaces and photonic crystal slabs, which support high-quality factor modes like bound states in the continuum (BICs), provide a promising alternative platform for enhancing light-matter interaction without intrinsic absorption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coupling the giant, ultrafast nonlinear susceptibility of ISBTs to the extreme field confinement and low loss of dielectric resonator modes, particularly symmetry-protected BICs, will yield unprecedented nonlinear conversion efficiencies and enable active tuning of nonlinear processes at mid-infrared frequencies.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate III-V semiconductor metasurfaces where multiple quantum well heterostructures are embedded within high-index dielectric (e.g., GaAs/AlGaAs) nanopillars or grating structures. Using full-wave electromagnetic simulations (e.g., finite-difference time-domain methods), we will engineer geometries to align the ISBT resonance (e.g., at ~10 μm) with a magnetic dipole Mie resonance or a guided-mode resonance BIC. Nonlinear optical characterization will involve pump-probe spectroscopy and frequency conversion measurements (e.g., second-harmonic generation) using a tunable quantum cascade laser source to map the enhanced nonlinear response and its tunability via applied bias or temperature.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for high-efficiency, active nonlinear nanophotonics in the mid-infrared, moving beyond lossy plasmonic paradigms. It will demonstrate a novel class of electro-optically tunable nonlinear metasurfaces with potential applications in on-chip frequency conversion, ultrafast optical modulation, and integrated sensing platforms.
proposals.novelty: While ISBT-plasmon coupling is well-studied, the integration of ISBTs with low-loss dielectric resonances and BICs for nonlinear optics is unexplored. The proposed synergy combines the best of both worlds: the exceptional nonlinearity of ISBTs and the superior photonic properties of dielectric metasurfaces.

proposals.title: Wafer-Scale Fabrication of Nonlinear Metasurfaces for Polarization-Engineered Thermal Emission
proposals.motivation: Nonlinear metasurfaces, particularly those enabling tailored thermal emission and optical isolation, have demonstrated groundbreaking control over light-matter interactions at the nanoscale. However, their translation to practical applications in energy harvesting, thermal management, and integrated photonics is severely hindered by fabrication limitations, as most demonstrations are confined to small, lab-scale areas with non-uniform performance. Achieving uniform, large-area fabrication is the critical next step to move these devices from proof-of-concept to real-world impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting and integrating advanced thin-film deposition and nanoimprint lithography techniques, we can achieve wafer-scale, uniform fabrication of nonlinear metasurfaces based on materials like twisted Weyl semimetals, preserving their critical polarization-dependent and nonreciprocal optical responses.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid fabrication process combining atomic layer deposition (ALD) for uniform, conformal growth of active nonlinear layers (e.g., Weyl semimetal thin films) on 4-inch silicon wafers. A master stamp with the metasurface pattern will be fabricated via electron-beam lithography and reactive ion etching. This stamp will then be used for step-and-repeat nanoimprint lithography (NIL) to pattern the wafer-scale substrate. Critical process parameters—including imprint pressure, temperature, and ALD cycle count—will be optimized and characterized using spectroscopic ellipsometry and spatially resolved Fourier-plane imaging to map emission polarization uniformity across the wafer.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable fabrication methodology for nonlinear metasurfaces, directly enabling their integration into macroscopic systems. It will provide the first experimental data on the uniformity and yield of wafer-scale nonreciprocal and polarization-engineered thermal emitters, bridging the gap between nanophotonic design and manufacturable devices.
