proposals.novelty: While nanoimprint lithography has been used for linear metasurfaces, its application to wafer-scale patterning of active, nonlinear materials like Weyl semimetals for tailored thermal emission is unexplored. The novelty lies in the co-optimization of large-area material growth and nanopatterning to preserve delicate nonlinear and nonreciprocal responses.

proposals.title: Nonlinearity as a Probe: Mapping Local Field Asymmetries and Material Properties via Harmonic Generation in Twisted Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Metasurfaces enable precise control of light-matter interactions, with recent work demonstrating strong nonlinear responses and tailored thermal emission. However, the inverse problem—using a measured nonlinear optical output as a sensitive probe of the local electromagnetic environment or subtle material asymmetries—remains largely unexplored. This approach could open new paradigms for ultra-compact, all-optical sensing and material characterization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficiency and polarization state of harmonic generation (e.g., second- or third-harmonic) in a metasurface with engineered symmetry breaking are exquisitely sensitive functions of local field enhancements and material tensor properties, enabling their quantitative reconstruction from far-field nonlinear measurements.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate plasmonic or dielectric metasurfaces with controlled in-plane asymmetry, inspired by twisted bilayer concepts, to support strong local field enhancements and tailored nonlinear polarization currents. We will develop an inverse design algorithm based on adjoint optimization, where the forward model couples full-wave electromagnetic simulations (e.g., FDTD) with a nonlinear susceptibility tensor model. The algorithm will iteratively adjust local material parameters in the simulation to minimize the difference between the simulated and experimentally measured nonlinear harmonic intensity and polarization patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general framework for nonlinear metasurface-based sensing, moving beyond using nonlinearity as a mere signal source to using it as a diagnostic tool. It will provide a new method to characterize local field distributions and material nonlinearities with sub-wavelength information extracted from far-field data.
proposals.novelty: While nonlinear metasurfaces are well-studied for wavefront shaping, their systematic application as a probe in an inverse problem setting is novel. The approach uniquely leverages engineered symmetry breaking—a hallmark of Alù's recent work on isolation and thermal emission—to create a nonlinear response explicitly tied to local asymmetries for sensing.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Nanophotonics at the Single-Meta-Atom Limit: Isolating Intrinsic Kerr and Thermal Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Metasurfaces have demonstrated remarkable control over linear wave phenomena, but their nonlinear optical properties are typically measured in ensembles, where collective effects and inhomogeneous broadening obscure the intrinsic response of the individual meta-atom. Understanding this fundamental, isolated response is critical for designing efficient nonlinear nanoscale devices, such as ultrafast optical switches and frequency converters, where performance is dictated by single-element physics rather than collective averaging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, high-quality-factor dielectric or plasmonic nanoresonator, when completely isolated from lattice coupling effects, will exhibit a nonlinear optical response—specifically a Kerr nonlinearity and associated thermal dynamics—that is fundamentally different and more efficient than the response measured in periodic arrays, due to the elimination of inter-element scattering and disorder.
proposals.method: We will fabricate isolated, high-index dielectric (e.g., silicon) or plasmonic (e.g., gold) nanoresonators on low-index substrates using electron-beam lithography. A tightly focused, wavelength-tunable laser beam will excite individual resonators in a scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscope (s-SNOM) setup. We will employ a dual-pump probe technique: a strong pump pulse to drive the nonlinearity and a weak, time-delayed probe to measure the transient shift in the resonant frequency and linewidth. Full-wave electromagnetic simulations (FDTD, Lumerical) coupled with a nonlinear perturbative model will be used to interpret the single-element polarizability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct measurement framework for the intrinsic nonlinear susceptibility of a single meta-atom, disentangling it from collective lattice effects. It will provide fundamental design rules for optimizing nonlinearity at the nanoscale and reveal the ultimate limits of nonlinear conversion efficiency in subwavelength resonators.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble nonlinear metasurface studies are common, directly probing and modeling the nonlinear dynamics of a single, isolated meta-atom—free from any photonic crystal or grating effects—is a novel and necessary step towards a bottom-up understanding of nonlinear nanophotonics.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Polarization-Driven Launching and Wavefront Shaping of Surface Plasmons
proposals.motivation: Efficiently launching and controlling surface plasmon polaritons (SPPs) is fundamental for ultra-compact photonic circuits and sensing. Traditional methods like prism or grating coupling are rigid and lack dynamic control. A scheme using optical nonlinearity to generate SPPs offers a pathway to all-optical, ultrafast, and reconfigurable plasmonic signal generation directly at subwavelength scales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nonlinear polarization induced in a non-centrosymmetric metasurface, when driven by tailored fundamental frequency waves, can act as a phased array of sources to efficiently launch and arbitrarily shape the wavefront of SPPs.
proposals.method: We will design a lithium niobate or gallium arsenide metasurface on a metal (gold) substrate, with unit cells engineered for a large second-order susceptibility (χ(2)). Using full-wave finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations, we will model the nonlinear polarization source term P(2ω) = ε0χ(2)E(ω)E(ω). By spatially modulating the phase of the incident fundamental beam (e.g., using a spatial light modulator), we will control the phase profile of the generated P(2ω) source array to launch SPPs with designed wavefronts, such as focused, vortex, or self-bending beams.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct nonlinear optical interface for generating and sculpting plasmonic fields, moving beyond linear scattering techniques. It will provide a framework for ultrafast all-optical switching and routing of SPPs, directly impacting the design of active plasmonic nanocircuits and on-chip signal processing.
proposals.novelty: While nonlinear metasurfaces have been studied for harmonic generation, their use as a phased array source for launching and wavefront-engineering SPPs is unexplored. This approach merges nonlinear nanophotonics with plasmonics in a uniquely dynamic and subwavelength platform.

proposals.title: Quantum Noise at Non-Hermitian Metamaterial Interfaces: An Input-Output Theory Framework
proposals.motivation: Metasurfaces and metamaterials enable unprecedented control over light, from thermal emission to optical isolation. However, their performance is fundamentally limited by intrinsic noise, including thermal fluctuations and quantum zero-point energy, especially near exceptional points or in non-Hermitian systems. A rigorous quantum model of this noise is lacking, hindering the design of low-noise photonic devices for sensing and communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the quantum input-output formalism to model finite metamaterial interfaces will reveal fundamental bounds on noise performance, directly linking non-Hermitian scattering properties to amplified quantum and thermal fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the input-output theory, standard in quantum optics for cavities and waveguides, to model finite metamaterial slabs and interfaces. The method involves quantizing the electromagnetic field in the presence of a spatially dispersive, lossy/gainy material described by a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian. We will derive quantum Langevin equations for the field operators, with noise operators enforcing commutation relations. This will yield a generalized scattering matrix that includes quantum noise correlations, applicable to structures like twisted Weyl semimetal isolators and thermal metasurfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully quantum model for noise in finite, non-Hermitian metamaterials, establishing design principles for minimizing noise in isolators, thermal emitters, and sensors. It will bridge the fields of quantum optics and metamaterial physics, offering a tool to predict quantum limits in active and time-varying metasystems.
proposals.novelty: While input-output theory is well-known, its application to model quantum noise in spatially complex, non-Hermitian metamaterial interfaces—moving beyond simple cavity QED—is novel. This directly addresses a key gap in understanding the quantum statistical properties of light engineered by metasurfaces.

proposals.title: Quantum Fluctuations and Nonlocal Effects in the Radiative Heat Transfer of Few-Unit-Cell Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: The effective medium description of metamaterials and metasurfaces, which underpins their exotic wave-control capabilities, is known to break down at deeply subwavelength scales where nonlocal and quantum effects become significant. Prior to 2026, the impact of these phenomena on radiative thermal processes—such as near-field heat transfer and thermal emission—remained largely unexplored, creating a critical knowledge gap for next-generation nanophotonic devices operating at the atomic-scale limit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in metasurfaces scaled down to a few unit cells, the breakdown of the local effective medium approximation will lead to measurable modifications in quantum noise spectra and near-field radiative heat transfer, governed by nonlocal optical responses and the emergence of discrete, quantum-fluctuating current sources.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate plasmonic and dielectric metasurfaces with a deliberately small number of repeating unit cells (2-10) using electron-beam lithography and transfer techniques. Their optical response will be characterized via scattering-type near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) and Fourier-plane thermal emission spectroscopy. Theoretical analysis will employ a fluctuational electrodynamics framework extended with a nonlocal, spatially dispersive dielectric model, and numerical simulations will be conducted using finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) methods incorporating stochastic dipole sources to model quantum noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the fundamental limits of classical effective-medium theories for thermal photonics and provide the first experimental signatures of quantum noise modification in ultra-compact metamaterials. It will deliver new design principles for quantum-fluctuation-driven devices and inform the scaling of coherent thermal sources and near-field energy harvesters.
proposals.novelty: While nonlocal effects have been studied in bulk metamaterials, their explicit connection to quantum fluctuations and radiative heat transfer in systems with extreme dimensional confinement—effectively bridging metasurface physics with quantum fluctuational electrodynamics—is a novel and unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Thermal Metasurfaces for Prescribed Photon Noise Statistics
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Alù's group and others has demonstrated precise control over thermal emission's polarization, directionality, and spectrum using metasurfaces. However, designing structures to achieve a *target statistical distribution* of noise photons—a critical resource for quantum sensing, thermal imaging, and radiative cooling—remains an open challenge. This proposal addresses the inverse problem: treating the inherent fluctuations of thermal radiation not as a nuisance, but as a tunable design parameter.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a gradient-based inverse-design framework, incorporating fluctuational electrodynamics, can discover non-intuitive metasurface geometries that generate thermal emission with user-defined second-order coherence properties (e.g., super- or sub-Planckian photon statistics) at specified frequencies and angles.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline combining finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations with the fluctuation-dissipation theorem to model thermal emission from arbitrary silicon-based metasurfaces. An adjoint-based optimization method will be implemented, where the objective function minimizes the difference between the calculated and target normalized second-order correlation function, g^(2)(ω, θ). The design degrees of freedom will be the binarized geometry of a 2D metasurface unit cell, optimized using a stochastic gradient descent algorithm (e.g., Adam) to navigate the complex landscape towards structures that sculpt thermal photon statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for the inverse design of thermal noise statistics, moving beyond average intensity control. It will provide a library of metasurface designs that produce thermal sources with tailored quantum-optical characteristics, directly impacting fields like secure thermal communication and background-limited detection. The project will demonstrate a new paradigm where noise is engineered as a functional element.
proposals.novelty: While inverse design is common for linear optical responses, its application to engineer the *statistical fluctuations* of thermal emission—a non-equilibrium, broadband phenomenon—is unprecedented. This shifts the design goal from the electromagnetic field to the quantum statistical properties of the field, a dual problem to conventional metasurface design.

proposals.title: Static Non-Reciprocal Thermal Emission via Synthetic Gauge Fields in Anisotropic Weyl Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Achieving non-reciprocity in optics is crucial for devices like isolators and circulators, but conventional methods rely on dynamic modulation or magnetic fields, which are bulky and challenging to integrate. A static, magnetless approach would revolutionize on-chip photonic systems. Recent work on twisted Weyl semimetals and arbitrarily polarized thermal emission from metasurfaces suggests a pathway to break reciprocity without time-varying elements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a static, planar metasurface composed of anisotropic, low-symmetry Weyl semimetal nanostructures can generate a synthetic gauge field for photons, leading to strongly non-reciprocal absorption and emission spectra at infrared wavelengths.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate metasurfaces using twisted bilayer graphene analogs and anisotropic transition metal dichalcogenides (e.g., WTe2) to emulate a static synthetic magnetic field. The design will leverage in-plane anisotropy and sharp resonances to couple to the thermal Rashba effect. Full-wave simulations (FDTD, RCWA) will be used to compute the non-reciprocal scattering matrix. Non-reciprocity will be experimentally quantified via a custom, dual-port Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy setup measuring the directional asymmetry in emissivity and absorptivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for static, on-chip optical non-reciprocity, directly applicable to thermal photonics and infrared signal processing. It will provide the first experimental demonstration of a synthetic gauge field for light in a fully passive, planar platform, linking concepts from topological condensed matter to radiative heat transfer.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its exclusive use of static, geometric/material anisotropy in 2D quantum materials to break reciprocity, moving beyond dynamic modulation or natural magneto-optical effects. It uniquely merges the concepts of synthetic gauge fields and engineered thermal emission.

proposals.title: Broadband Optical Non-Reciprocity via Spatiotemporally Engineered Metasurface Modulation
proposals.motivation: Time-modulated photonic systems offer a powerful, magnet-free route to optical non-reciprocity and isolation, crucial for integrated photonic circuits. However, state-of-the-art implementations (pre-2026) are fundamentally limited to narrow operational bandwidths due to their reliance on resonant modulation schemes, which hinders practical applications. Overcoming this bandwidth bottleneck is a critical, unmet challenge in non-Hermitian and topological photonics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by implementing a traveling-wave spatiotemporal modulation profile across a non-resonant, gradient metasurface interface, we can induce a broadband non-reciprocal phase shift, decoupling the isolation bandwidth from the narrow linewidth of any single resonant element.
proposals.method: We will design a dielectric metasurface with a linear phase gradient for beam steering. Instead of uniformly modulating its resonance, we will apply a spatially varying electro-optic modulation, creating a traveling-wave perturbation that sweeps across the interface at a velocity matching the phase gradient. This couples the spatial and temporal degrees of freedom. We will use full-wave FDTD simulations coupled with a Floquet scattering matrix analysis to model the system. The modulation waveform will be optimized via adjoint-based inverse design to maximize the forward-backward transmission contrast over a multi-octave frequency range in the near-infrared.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a theoretical framework and a practical design principle for achieving broadband magnetless optical isolation, directly addressing a key limitation in the field. It will demonstrate a novel class of spatiotemporally modulated metasurfaces that can control reciprocity over wide bandwidths, enabling new functionalities in integrated photonic systems, such as broadband circulators and isolators.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of a non-resonant phase-gradient metasurface with a traveling-wave spatiotemporal modulation, moving beyond localized resonant modulation to achieve a distributed, momentum-based non-reciprocal response that is inherently broadband.

proposals.title: Nonreciprocal Photonic Transport in a Single High-Q Nanocavity via Dynamically Modulated Synthetic Gauge Fields
proposals.motivation: Achieving optical nonreciprocity—where light transmission differs for forward and backward propagation—without magnetic materials is a central challenge in integrated photonics. While synthetic gauge fields created by spatiotemporal modulation in waveguide arrays have shown promise, these systems are bulky and lossy, hindering on-chip integration. Scaling this physics down to a single, ultra-compact resonator would enable fundamental studies of non-Hermitian topology and unlock novel isolators and circulators for nanophotonic circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying tailored spatiotemporal modulation to the resonant modes of a single, high-quality-factor (high-Q) photonic crystal nanocavity, we can synthesize an effective magnetic flux for photons, leading to measurable nonreciprocal frequency shifts and isolation within a single resonant element.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate high-Q (Q > 10^5) L3-type photonic crystal cavities in silicon-on-insulator. Nonreciprocity will be induced via dynamic modulation: we will pattern metallic microheaters or PIN junctions adjacent to the cavity to create a traveling-wave refractive index perturbation via radio-frequency signals, mimicking a synthetic gauge field. The system will be characterized using a balanced, frequency-swept interferometric setup to separately probe forward and backward excitation, measuring the splitting of clockwise and counterclockwise resonant modes as a function of modulation phase and amplitude.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first implementation of a synthetic gauge field confined to a single nanophotonic resonator, establishing a new platform for studying non-Hermitian topological physics at the fundamental mode level. It will provide a blueprint for ultra-compact, magnet-free optical isolators and circulators that are compatible with dense photonic integration, advancing toward full on-chip optical processing.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel because it transcends the paradigm of using coupled resonator arrays or waveguides for synthetic magnetism, condensing the effect into a single, wavelength-scale cavity. It uniquely combines the concepts of dynamic modulation, previously applied to extended structures, with the extreme field confinement and high coherence of a single nanoresonator.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Self-Induced Gauge Potentials for Dynamically Reconfigurable Light Routing
proposals.motivation: Synthetic gauge fields in photonics, often engineered via static structural twists or modulation, enable precise control over light propagation, such as unidirectional emission and isolation. However, these fields are typically fixed post-fabrication, limiting real-time adaptability. A system where light itself can dynamically reconfigure its gauge field would enable fundamentally new optical functionalities, such as intensity-dependent routing and self-steering beams, advancing adaptive photonic circuits and optical computing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a nonlinear optical metasurface, incorporating materials like epsilon-near-zero (ENZ) media or III-V semiconductors, can generate a self-induced synthetic gauge field where the intensity and polarization of an incident optical signal nonlinearly modify the effective magnetic flux experienced by the signal itself, leading to dynamic control over its propagation path.
proposals.method: We will design a plasmonic or dielectric metasurface lattice with a broken inversion symmetry to host a static synthetic gauge field. This lattice will be embedded within a Kerr-type nonlinear material (e.g., AlGaAs). Using full-wave nonlinear simulations (e.g., FDTD with nonlinear coupled-mode theory), we will model how high-intensity incident light alters the local refractive index, thereby modifying the phase accumulation (Peierls phase) for light circulating in the lattice's meta-atoms. The key metric is the intensity-dependent shift in the system's photonic band structure and associated Chern number, which dictates edge state propagation. Experimental validation will use near-field scanning optical microscopy (NSOM) to map the evolution of edge state trajectories under varying pump intensities.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm of nonlinear nonreciprocal photonics, where optical nonreciprocity becomes self-induced and intensity-tunable, moving beyond magnet-based or linear time-modulated schemes. It will provide a foundational framework for designing optical isolators, circulators, and routers whose operation is controlled all-optically, without external magnetic fields or RF modulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Alù and others has used external stimuli to tune gauge fields, the concept of a self-induced, nonlinear gauge field where the signal is both the probe and the control parameter is unexplored. This merges the concepts of nonlinear topological photonics and dynamic gauge fields in a fundamentally new way.

proposals.title: Full-Waveform Inversion for Broadband Transparency in Dispersive Metamaterials
proposals.motivation: Understanding and designing transparency in strongly dispersive metamaterials and plasmonic structures remains a challenge. Current approaches often rely on monochromatic analysis or effective medium theories, which fail to capture the causal, broadband nature of transparency dictated by Kramers-Kronig relations. A method to rigorously reconstruct the spatial dispersive profile from broadband scattering data is critically needed to move beyond these simplified models.
proposals.hypothesis: The full-waveform inversion (FWI) framework from seismic imaging, which explicitly models frequency-dependent attenuation and dispersion, can be adapted to electromagnetics to uniquely reconstruct the spatial distribution of complex, dispersive constitutive parameters (ε(ω), μ(ω)) and define true broadband transparency.
proposals.method: We will illuminate a dispersive object (e.g., a plasmonic nanoparticle cluster or a dielectric metasurface) with a tailored, ultra-wideband electromagnetic pulse. The scattered and transmitted fields will be measured across frequency and angle. An adjoint-based FWI solver will be implemented, where the forward model solves Maxwell's equations with realistic dispersion models (e.g., multi-pole Lorentz). The inverse problem will iteratively minimize the misfit between simulated and measured waveforms using a gradient descent algorithm (e.g., L-BFGS) to reconstruct maps of the object's dispersive parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, causality-respecting framework for defining and analyzing transparency in dispersive photonic systems. It will yield a novel computational tool for the non-destructive characterization of complex metamaterials and metasurfaces, directly linking their broadband scattering response to their internal dispersive structure.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of the geophysical FWI framework—with its explicit handling of viscoelastic dispersion—to electromagnetic inverse scattering for dispersive media is unprecedented. It challenges the photonics community's standard monochromatic and effective-medium analyses by enforcing a full broadband, causal reconstruction.

proposals.title: Dispersion-Engineered Transparency: Tailoring Scattering Cancellation for Core Objects with Strong Material Dispersion
proposals.motivation: Achieving broadband scattering cancellation and transparency for objects with inherent, strong material dispersion remains a significant challenge in wave physics. Pre-2026 work on cloaking and mantle cloaking often assumes weak or negligible dispersion, limiting practical applications to narrowband operation for realistic materials like polar dielectrics or semiconductors near resonances. This research addresses the fundamental wave-matter interaction problem of designing a metasurface interface that can compensate for the complex frequency-dependent permittivity of a dispersive core.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a passive, non-resonant metasurface shell, designed with a multi-pole scattering cancellation model that explicitly incorporates the Kramers-Kronig consistent dispersion of the core object, can achieve tailored transparency windows and scattering suppression across a designed bandwidth, even when the core's material properties vary strongly with frequency.
proposals.method: We will develop a semi-analytical scattering matrix model based on Mie theory, where the dispersive properties of the core (e.g., described by a Lorentz or Drude model) are explicitly included. The metasurface shell will be modeled as a zero-thickness impedance boundary, and its required surface conductivity profile will be inversely engineered using a multi-frequency optimization algorithm (e.g., adjoint-based optimization) to cancel the dominant electric and magnetic dipole moments of the core-shell system across a target band. We will validate the design using full-wave finite-element simulations (COMSOL) for a spherical silicon carbide (SiC) core near its Reststrahlen band and fabricate a prototype using patterned graphene metasurfaces on a dielectric spacer for experimental verification in the mid-infrared.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general theoretical framework for dispersion-aware cloaking, moving beyond the single-frequency quasi-static approximation. It will provide a recipe for designing broadband transparent interfaces for inherently dispersive objects, with direct applications in mitigating thermal emission signatures, protecting sensors from scattering, and enabling new non-reciprocal devices when combined with magneto-optical effects.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of fundamental material dispersion laws into the metasurface inverse design process for scattering cancellation, explicitly targeting bandwidth limitations imposed by causality (Kramers-Kronig relations), which is a non-trivial extension of previous frequency-independent or narrowband mantle cloaking theories.

proposals.title: Giant Radar Cross-Section Enhancement via Mie-Resonant Photonic Crystal Coatings
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in radar engineering and metamaterials research is to minimize scattering for stealth. However, applications in radar calibration, safety beacons, and decoy technology require the opposite: a controlled, dramatic enhancement of scattering from a small object. Existing methods for increasing radar cross-section (RCS), like corner reflectors, are bulky and narrowband. A resonant, planar, and tunable solution would enable new functionalities in radar systems and electromagnetic signature control.
proposals.hypothesis: A planar, multi-layered photonic crystal coating, designed with sub-wavelength periodicity and tailored material dispersion, can support engineered Mie resonances that constructively interfere in the backscattering direction, leading to orders-of-magnitude enhancement of an object's monostatic radar cross-section at specific frequency bands.
proposals.method: We will design a coating comprising alternating dielectric layers (e.g., Si/SiO2) with a supercell periodicity to create a photonic bandgap and defect modes that couple to the Mie resonances of the coated object. Full-wave simulations (using COMSOL Multiphysics or CST Studio Suite) will optimize the layer thicknesses and lattice constants for constructive interference in the backscattered field at S- and X-band radar frequencies. Fabrication will use standard thin-film deposition and lithography on a substrate. The scattering enhancement will be characterized in an anechoic chamber using a vector network analyzer and a calibrated radar measurement setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for electromagnetic signature *enhancement*, complementing the vast literature on scattering suppression. It will deliver a thin-film, frequency-agile platform for generating giant radar signatures, with direct applications in calibration targets and active decoys. Furthermore, it will bridge concepts from optical Mie-tronics and thermal emission control to the radar domain.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the core objective of photonic crystal and metasurface cloaking for selective, resonant *brightening*. It uniquely combines the resonant field enhancement of Mie scatterers with the wavevector-selective reflection of a 1D photonic crystal to achieve directional, giant backscattering from a planar interface, a non-obvious synergy.

proposals.title: Spatio-Temporal Illumination Engineering for Sub-Diffraction Thermal Gradient Control: A Quantitative Benchmark Against Plane-Wave Limits
proposals.motivation: Recent work on thermal metasurfaces has demonstrated unprecedented control over thermal emission polarization and directionality. However, the fundamental limits of spatio-temporal excitation for controlling nanoscale thermal energy deposition and transient thermal gradients remain unexplored. A rigorous, quantitative benchmark is needed to determine when complex, tailored excitations outperform conventional plane-wave illumination for specific nanoscale tasks, moving beyond qualitative demonstrations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimally engineered spatio-temporal excitations, combining tailored near-field spatial profiles and ultrafast temporal pulse shaping, can significantly outperform optimized single-frequency plane waves in creating and controlling sub-diffraction-limited thermal gradients in a nanostructured material.
proposals.method: We will define a target metric: the spatial localization and temporal contrast of a thermal gradient in a lossy dielectric nanostructure. Using a coupled electro-thermal solver, we will employ a gradient-based inverse design algorithm (e.g., adjoint optimization) to find the optimal multi-frequency, near-field excitation profile. This will be generated via a theoretical metasurface coupler. Performance will be quantified and compared against the best-case single-frequency plane wave, optimized for the same metric under identical constraints on total incident energy and material properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first definitive, quantitative framework for assessing the value of complex illumination in nanoscale thermal management. It will establish clear performance bounds and identify the regimes where spatio-temporal engineering offers decisive advantages, guiding future experiments in thermal photonics and near-field energy control.
proposals.novelty: While metasurface-based wavefront shaping is established, a direct, rigorous benchmark of spatio-temporal excitations against the plane-wave standard for a concrete thermal metric is novel. This shifts the focus from capability demonstration to quantitative advantage assessment.

proposals.title: Transient Topological Band Engineering in Large-Scale Mechanical Metamaterials for Seismic Pulse Shaping
proposals.motivation: Conventional seismic isolation strategies are challenged by the broadband, transient, and spatially complex nature of real earthquake wavefields, which contain high-energy pulses and near-field surface waves. Topological metamaterials offer a powerful paradigm for wave control, but their application to seismology has been limited to idealized, steady-state plane-wave analyses. There is a critical need to design metamaterials whose topological properties are explicitly tailored for the impulsive, multi-modal excitation of seismic events.
proposals.hypothesis: By engineering the transient response of topological mechanical metamaterials with orbital angular momentum (OAM) band structures, we can achieve selective, frequency-dependent attenuation and guidance of specific seismic pulse components (e.g., initial P-wave pulses, Love waves) that dominate near-field ground motion damage.
proposals.method: We will design large-scale, architectured mechanical metamaterials using unit cells that support OAM modes, analogous to optical metasurfaces. The transient topological band structure will be analyzed via time-domain finite-element simulations (e.g., COMSOL) under broadband, impulsive excitations replicating seismic records. We will employ optimization algorithms (e.g., adjoint-based topology optimization) to tailor the geometric dispersion and coupling between OAM modes to maximize energy transfer from damaging seismic frequencies into topologically protected, guided edge states or radiative channels within the metamaterial foundation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new framework for performance-based seismic design based on transient topological wave physics, moving beyond static bandgap concepts. It will deliver specific metamaterial blueprints for architectural-scale foundations that selectively mitigate the most damaging components of a seismic wavefield, validated through scaled experimental models.
proposals.novelty: This proposal fundamentally shifts the analysis of topological metamaterials for seismology from steady-state to transient dynamics and explicitly targets the complex spatial wavefronts (near-field, surface waves) of real earthquakes, a non-obvious extension of metasurface concepts from optics to large-scale mechanics.

proposals.title: Nonlinear and Near-Field Dynamics of Twisted Weyl Metasurfaces for Pulsed and Evanescent-Wave Control
proposals.motivation: Metasurfaces and Weyl semimetal platforms have demonstrated exceptional wavefront control under ideal, monochromatic plane-wave illumination. However, real-world applications in ultrafast optics, thermal management, and quantum photonics require operation under pulsed excitation or near-field coupling from localized sources. The performance and fundamental limits of these systems under such non-ideal, broadband, and strongly confined conditions remain largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nonlocal and nonlinear optical response of twisted Weyl semimetals, combined with tailored metasurface dispersion, can be engineered to achieve robust, ultrafast isolation and tailored emission even under femtosecond pulsed excitation or when driven by evanescent near-fields, transcending the limitations of single-frequency plane-wave designs.
proposals.method: We will design and simulate bilayer twisted Weyl semimetal metasurfaces using full-wave finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) methods. The temporal response to femtosecond Gaussian pulses will be analyzed via Fourier-transform techniques to quantify isolation bandwidth and pulse distortion. Near-field excitation will be modeled using dipole sources and scanning near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) simulation protocols, evaluating the coupling efficiency and polarization sorting capabilities for evanescent waves. The analysis will focus on the interplay between geometric phase, material dispersion, and pulse bandwidth.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental framework for assessing metasurface performance in time-domain and near-field regimes. It will provide specific design rules for broadband optical isolators compatible with ultrafast lasers and for metasurfaces that interact efficiently with localized emitters (e.g., quantum dots, thermal spots), directly impacting integrated photonic circuits and radiative heat transfer technologies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimized for CW plane waves, this proposal systematically investigates the *temporal* and *spatial* spectral decomposition of metasurface functionality, a critical step toward practical deployment. The combination of pulsed dynamics with the unique nonlocal response of Weyl semimetals for isolation is a previously unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Circuit-Analog Metasurfaces for Tailored Thermal Emission: A Transmission-Line Approach to Polarization and Directivity Control
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including by Alù's group, has demonstrated unprecedented control over thermal emission using metasurfaces, achieving arbitrary polarization states and unidirectional beams. However, the design of these complex, multi-layered plasmonic coatings often relies on full-wave numerical simulations, which are computationally intensive and provide limited intuitive insight into the coupling between near-field plasmonic modes and far-field radiation patterns. A more efficient and physics-driven design framework is needed to accelerate the discovery of thermal metasurfaces with targeted functionalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optical response of layered thermal metasurfaces can be accurately modeled and engineered using cascaded transmission-line (TL) circuits, where each meta-atom layer and the emission interface are represented by lumped elements and impedance networks. This approach will directly link geometric and material parameters to the polarization and directivity of the emitted thermal field.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-port TL model for the unit cell of a layered plasmonic metasurface. Each resonant layer (e.g., a twisted Weyl semimetal film or a patterned metal-dielectric stack) will be mapped to an equivalent RLC circuit, with parameters extracted from analytical models or low-volume simulations. The far-field radiation condition will be modeled as a complex load impedance. We will employ transfer matrix methods and impedance matching theory to analytically derive conditions for circular polarization, unidirectional emission, and prescribed spin-orbit coupling. The circuit models will be validated against full-wave FDTD simulations for structures from recent publications, such as those demonstrating the thermal Rashba effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a powerful, intuitive, and computationally lightweight design tool for advanced thermal metasurfaces, moving beyond brute-force optimization. It will deliver closed-form design rules linking material properties and layer geometry to macroscopic emission properties. The resulting framework will significantly accelerate the inverse design of compact, multi-functional coatings for thermal management, spectroscopy, and secure infrared communication.
proposals.novelty: While circuit models are established for microwave metamaterials and simple metasurfaces, their rigorous application to model the intricate interplay of plasmonic resonances, thermal excitation, and polarization conversion in multi-layered structures for tailored thermal emission is novel. This bridges a gap between intuitive circuit design and the cutting-edge wavefront control demonstrated in recent thermal emission papers.

ideas.title: Orbital-Selective Scattering Coatings for Spectral and Directional Signature Control
ideas.description: This research direction combines the principle of orbital-selective topology via non-degenerate resonant modes with the goal of enhanced scattering. The result is a resonant coating or metasurface designed not for transparency, but to act as a spectral amplifier for specific, narrowband electronic or vibronic transitions in a target material or molecule adsorbed on its surface. By intentionally splitting and tuning resonant modes to match distinct orbital energy levels, the coating can selectively and dramatically boost the scattering cross-section of one chosen transition while leaving others unaffected. This enables new applications in ultra-sensitive, label-free chemical detection, active control of a material's radiative properties, and the dynamic engineering of object signatures in specific spectral bands.

ideas.title: Transient Topological Metamaterials for Seismic Pulse and Near-Field Wave Mitigation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the design of large-scale mechanical metamaterials with orbital angular momentum (OAM) topology specifically optimized for the transient, broadband, and spatially complex nature of seismic wavefields. It challenges the conventional assumption of steady-state, plane-wave excitation by analyzing and tailoring the topological band structure's response to impulsive seismic pulses and near-field ground motions, which contain strong surface waves and spatially decaying fields. The goal is to develop architectural-scale metamaterial foundations or inclusions that can selectively and adaptively attenuate, guide, or scatter the most damaging components of a real seismic event, such as the high-energy initial P-wave pulse or the destructive Love waves, offering a new frontier in performance-based seismic design.

ideas.title: Quantitative performance benchmarking of optimized spatio-temporal excitation for target metrics against the plane-wave standard
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a generic challenge to a rigorous, quantitative comparison. It first defines a specific nanoscale task, such as breaking the diffraction limit in label-free imaging or controlling thermal gradients in a material. A target metric is established, and an optimal excitation scheme—combining tailored temporal pulse shaping and engineered near-field spatial profiles—is designed theoretically and computationally to maximize this metric. The system's performance is then benchmarked against an optimized single-frequency plane-wave illumination under identical conditions, providing a definitive, quantifiable assessment of the advantages and trade-offs of complex illumination strategies.

ideas.title: Resonant Mie Scattering Enhancement via Photonic Crystal Coatings
ideas.description: This idea inverts the common stealth goal of transparency to one of enhanced scattering, drawing an analogy from astrophysics where faint dust clouds are studied via their enhanced scattering of starlight. The analogous solution uses a known light source and the cloud's resonant properties to amplify a weak signal. Adapted to radar, the research proposes applying a resonant, multi-layered photonic crystal coating to an object. The coating's periodicity and material composition are designed to support strong Mie resonances at specific radar bands, creating constructive interference in the back-scattered waves. This would dramatically increase the object's radar scattering cross-section, making it appear as a much larger, brighter target, which could be useful for decoy generation, radar calibration, or ensuring detectability for safety.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Waveform Inversion for Dispersive Transparency Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel problem in geophysics—imaging the Earth's interior using dispersed seismic waves—to challenge assumptions about transparency in objects with strong material dispersion. In seismology, full-waveform inversion (FWI) successfully reconstructs subsurface structures by explicitly modeling frequency-dependent attenuation and velocity dispersion (e.g., via viscoelastic Q models) and solving a large-scale inverse problem that matches simulated to observed broadband waveforms. Adapted to photonics or electromagnetics, this approach would involve illuminating a core object with known, strong frequency-dependent material properties (e.g., a metamaterial, plasmonic structure, or dispersive dielectric) using a broadband electromagnetic pulse. The complex transmitted and scattered fields would be measured. An inverse solver, incorporating realistic dispersion models (e.g., Drude-Lorentz, Debye), would then be iteratively optimized to reconstruct the spatial profile of the object's dispersive constitutive parameters. This method challenges the assumption that transparency for such objects can be understood through monochromatic or effective-medium theories, proposing instead that true 'transparency' is a broadband, causal phenomenon requiring reconstruction via full-waveform inversion techniques borrowed from geophysics.

ideas.title: Adapting Computational Conformal Mapping for Complex-Shaped Transparency Coatings
ideas.description: This expanded research idea applies an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics to solve the challenge of extending transparency coating concepts to non-spherical, non-cylindrical geometries. In aerodynamics, simulating flow over complex aircraft shapes is tackled by computationally mapping the complex surface to a simpler canonical domain (like a sphere), solving the equations there, and mapping back. Adapting this solution, we propose to develop a computational framework for designing transformation-optics-based transparency coatings for arbitrary 3D forms. The method would involve creating a numerical conformal or quasi-conformal map from the surface of the complex target object (e.g., an ellipsoid, polyhedron, or arbitrary 3D mesh) to a simple reference shape. The known coating solution for the reference shape is then transformed via this mapping to generate the precise, graded index profile or metamaterial structure required on the actual complex object, enabling a systematic design approach for cloaking or transparency effects on arbitrarily shaped items.

ideas.title: Applying Antenna Array Synthesis Methods to Coating Parameter Optimization
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from antenna array synthesis to systematically optimize coating parameters. In antenna design, synthesis techniques like Chebyshev or Taylor distributions are used to determine element parameters (spacing, amplitude) to achieve a specific radiation pattern. Analogously, for a multi-layer coating applied to radar or sensing, each layer can be treated as an element in a stack. The coating's desired frequency response—such as a specific radar absorption profile or a sensing resonance—is defined as the target 'pattern.' Established array synthesis algorithms and iterative optimization routines are then adapted to work with electromagnetic propagation models through layered media. This provides an inverse design methodology to calculate the optimal sequence of layer thicknesses and material permittivities required for the practical application, moving beyond trial-and-error parameter sweeps.

ideas.title: Architectural-scale orbital metamaterials inspired by cochlear frequency separation
ideas.description: This research adapts the cochlea's solution for frequency separation and damping to architectural-scale vibration control. The cochlea uses a tonotopic map along the basilar membrane, where graded mechanical properties (stiffness, mass) cause different frequencies to peak at specific locations, enabling frequency-specific mechanical filtering. We propose designing large-scale mechanical metamaterials with orbital topology where the geometric parameters of the orbital mechanisms (linkage sizes, pivot stiffnesses, rotational constraints) are systematically graded across a structural component. This creates a built-in mechanical frequency map: different zones of a building frame or bridge support would be tuned to absorb and dissipate energy from specific vibration frequency bands (e.g., low-frequency seismic waves, mid-frequency traffic vibrations, high-frequency wind loads). The orbital topology provides the reconfigurable, nonlinear mechanical response needed for this graded tuning, scaling cochlear-inspired passive frequency separation to protect civil engineering structures.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Imaging's Source Encoding to Challenge Single-Frequency Plane-Wave Assumptions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption of single-frequency, plane-wave illumination in fields like optical microscopy or radar imaging by drawing an analogy from seismic exploration geophysics. In seismics, the analogous problem of slow, single-source surveys with limited illumination angles was solved via 'source encoding,' where multiple sources fire with coded sequences, and the blended data is computationally deblended. Adapting this solution, the research proposes to illuminate a target with an array of near-field sources using pulsed, orthogonally-coded excitations (in time, frequency, or phase). Advanced computational inversion and deconvolution algorithms would then be developed to reconstruct the sample's response from this rich, superimposed dataset. This approach moves beyond the simplified plane-wave model, using engineered, complex illumination to enhance resolution, improve sensitivity to sub-wavelength features, and capture dynamic material properties that are averaged out or missed under standard illumination conditions.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Energy Landscapes to Acoustic Lattice Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the energy landscape theory from computational biochemistry, specifically protein folding, to the design of complex acoustic lattices. In protein folding, the final 3D structure emerges from navigating a high-dimensional energy landscape shaped by fundamental interactions. Analogously, we propose treating the resonant modes of discrete acoustic elements as basis states. By constructing a designed coupling 'energy landscape' for the lattice—where parameters like inter-element distance, coupling medium, and geometric symmetry act as the tunable 'interactions'—we can guide the system toward stable, pre-determined 'hybridized' states. These states correspond to specific acoustic band structures, analogous to sp, sp2, or sp3 orbital hybrids. Computational search and optimization algorithms, similar to those used in protein structure prediction, can then be employed to engineer physical lattice parameters that realize these target hybridized acoustic couplings, enabling precise control over wave propagation and dispersion.

ideas.title: Fault-Tolerant Hybrid Metamaterial Systems with Embedded Orbital Topology and Real-Time Integrity Management for Civil Infrastructure
ideas.description: This research designs a resilient vibration control system for civil engineering by embedding modular mechanical metamaterials with orbital topology within a conventional, robust structural framework. A distributed sensor network and control algorithm continuously assess the health and performance of both the conventional structure and the metamaterial dampers. The system dynamically reconfigures load paths and activates redundant orbital damper modules to maintain target damping performance, even if individual metamaterial elements degrade or fail. This approach ensures the reliability and safety required for seismic and architectural applications, making the advanced topological properties practically viable at scale.

ideas.title: Topology-Aware Digital Twins for Adaptive Seismic Protection of Large-Scale Metamaterial Structures
ideas.description: This research direction develops a new class of smart civil infrastructure by integrating large-scale mechanical metamaterials with orbital topology for vibration control with a real-time topological data analysis (TDA) engine. The TDA module processes field sensor data (e.g., accelerometer arrays) to automatically compute persistent homology signatures, classifying the operational topological band structure and identifying emerging localized modes or bulk-edge transitions induced by seismic activity or damage. This classification then feeds into an adaptive control system that physically reconfigures the metamaterial's lattice parameters or boundary conditions, dynamically shifting its orbital topology to suppress the identified dangerous modes. The result is a resilient, self-optimizing architectural or bridge system that can autonomously alter its fundamental wave-propagation properties in real-time to mitigate earthquakes.

ideas.title: Topological States from Non-Orthogonal Bases via Overcomplete Signal Representations
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption that a orthogonal basis is required to define topological invariants in electronic systems. By drawing an analogy from signal processing, where overcomplete, non-orthogonal frames are used for stable signal representation, we propose to design quantum systems where the orbital basis states are non-orthogonal. The analogous solution is to impose a 'frame condition' on the basis—generalizing the notion of completeness and stability—and to use the associated frame operator and dual frame to define the Berry connection and curvature. The adapted research idea is to construct tight-binding models from such a non-orthogonal frame, derive the conditions under which topological band gaps and robust edge states emerge, and formulate the correct expressions for topological invariants like the Chern number in this generalized non-orthogonal orbital basis.
